Modeling Results

We used logistic regression and exponentiated the model results, so that each coefficient is the multiplicative increase/decrease in the odds of diarrhea for that variable, compared to the baseline.

  • Incidence Count of Diarrhea With Wealth Index
  • Prevelence Count of Diarrhea With Wealth Index
  • Incdidence Count of Diarreha with Adjusted Wealth Index
  • Prevalence Count of Diarreha with Adjusted Wealth Index

The only variable that as a statistically significant association with diarrhea (incidence count) is wealth index. For an increase of 1 unit of the wealth index, the odds of diarrhea decrease by about 26%, accounting for all other variables. In other words, the higher the wealth index is, the lower the odds of diarrhea. We cannot make any conclusions about the other variable included in the model.

The only variable that as a statistically significant association with diarrhea (Prevalence count) is wealth index. For an increase of 1 unit of the wealth index, the odds of diarrhea decrease by about 29%, accounting for all other variables. In other words, the higher the wealth index is, the lower the odds of diarrhea. We cannot make any conclusions about the other variable included in the model.

The only variable that has a statistically significant association with diarrhea (incidence) in this model is whether or not the infants house floods. We see that the odds of diarrhea increase by 156% when an infants house floods. We cannot make any conclusions about the other variable included in the model.

There are three variables that have statistically significant associations with diarrhea (prevalence count): adjusted wealth index and water source: public tap (as compared to water source: inside home).

The odds of diarrhea decrease by about 36% for each increase in adjusted wealth index. The odds of diarrhea decrease by about 65% for households where water is from a public tap, as compared to water from the house.

Bayes Factor:

We used Bayes factors to jointly test all the components of the wealth index that were separated out when creating the adjusted wealth index, namely fridge, flooding, water source, no hand wash station, floor type and compound ground material. Large Bayes factors indicate that these separated variables are highly important in predicting incidence or prevalence.

Estimated Bayes factor in favor of incidence_mdl_adj over incidence_mdl_adj_null: 66253.06547
Estimated Bayes factor in favor of prevelence_mdl_adj over prevelence_mdl_adj_null: 7981.43316

Both Bayes Factors are very large, >1000, indicating that the separated variables are very important for predicting incidence and prevalence.
